session_notes.note: I smoke bidis. About 25 a day for the last 45 years. I've reduced a little now because the doctor here keeps telling me to.
Waking up in the morning is the hardest for me. That's when I usually have one first. Stopping it then is toughest.
Drinking water... yeah, I do that sometimes. But it doesn't really help me not think about smoking.
I can try. It's doable. But what if the cravings come back while I'm walking?
Maybe... I don't know. I just don't want to make him worry.

smoking_status_history: bidis for around 300 rupees a month
motivation_reason: need to find a way to deal with the situation on site

past_strategies.strategy: stopped smoking for a few days

session_notes.note: I smoke bidis. One packet a day, about 15 to 16. I've been doing this since I was around 18 or 20.
It happens most when I'm working on site, my coworkers smoke and offer me one. They say, "Have one, yaar," and it becomes easy to give in.

To be honest, my wife's been on my case lately about it. She wants to save money, and it's true, I spend a lot on bidis, around 300 rupees a month. And I guess it's also true that it's not good for me, but...

I don't know... I mean, I've tried quitting before, but it didn't work. And it's not like I can just stop because I want to, I need to find a way to deal with the situation on site. My coworkers smoke all the time, it's not like I can just sit there doing nothing.
Yeah, okay... I can try saying that. But what if they ask why, and I have to say "nothing"? It feels like a lie.

My brother's actually been bugging me to quit for a while. He's not a smoker, and he's always saying I'm being crazy for smoking so much. Maybe I could tell him I'm trying to quit, and he could be some support. But what if he just says "I told you so"?
I guess... having his support might actually help me stay on track. It's not like I've got anyone else to turn to, and if he's on my side, it might make a difference.

I'll just be honest with him, tell him how I feel about quitting and why I'm struggling. I don't know... maybe he'll just tell me to be strong and quit already, but maybe he'll actually listen and offer some advice or help.

I've tried quitting a couple of times before, but I never really had a plan. I just stopped smoking for a few days and then went back to it. This time, I think I need a better plan, and maybe my brother can help me come up with one.
I smoke bidis and eat paan. Started when I was around 25. Now it's maybe 7 or 8 bidis a day, down from more before.
Honestly, it's when I wake up, especially if I'm lying awake. It helps calm me down a bit, I guess.
My daughter brought me here. She says it's not good for my health, and I believe her. I want to be healthy, for her. And maybe it'll help me sleep better, too.
I used to like reading, but I don't do that much anymore. Maybe I could start reading again. It used to calm me down, you know?
I'm not sure... it's hard to get started, you know? But maybe I can try. I don't want to get up too early, though. That's when I usually have a bidi.
Making a cup of tea... that's easy. I can do that. But it's not just the tea, it's the bidi afterwards. That's what's hard.
Thank you. I'll try.
I would like that. For my daughter's sake.
My son is being treated here, so I thought I should see someone too. I smoke when I get angry or stressed. It's hard to explain.
About 10, maybe a bit more when things get really tough.
My wife says it would be healthier, but I'm not so sure. I mean, it helps me calm down when I'm angry or stressed. What else can I do?
I go for walks, yeah. It helps a bit, but it's not the same as smoking. I just seem to feel better after a cigarette, you know?
I do chew gum sometimes, but it doesn't do much. My mind's already made up to have a cigarette. I don't know, it's just a habit, I guess.

No, I just chew it and then spit it out. I don't know about that other way. Look, can we talk about something else? You're always asking about the gum and walks, what about my anger problem? Why do I keep smoking when I'm angry, that's the real question.
I think I would be more in control, I guess. I wouldn't yell at the kids, and I wouldn't take things out on my wife. But how do I do that? I don't know, I feel like a mess when I get angry.

Well, there was a time when my wife and I were at a school play with our son, and we had a disagreement about how to handle a situation with the teacher. But instead of smoking, I just took some deep breaths and walked around the block. When I came back, I felt better. But that was just one time, and I don't know how to make that happen all the time.
Yeah, I guess I can imagine it, but it's not the same as actually doing it. And it's hard to control when I get angry, you know? It just happens.
Honestly, I don't know why I'm here. I don't think smoking is a big deal. I've been doing it for 25 years and I'm fine.

My wife is always on my case about it, but I don't feel like it's affecting me that much. I can still do my job, I don't get as tired as I used to, and I've always been active, playing cricket in my youth.

I suppose I'd rather not have her nagging me about it anymore, to be honest. She's always on my case about something, and this feels like one of those things where she's trying to tell me how to live my life.

It's when we're just sitting at home after tea, having a quiet morning, and she starts bringing it up again. Or when I'm stressed out because of the shop, and she tells me I should quit smoking to calm down. It feels like she's not listening to me, like she's just trying to fix me.

I guess... we could do something else together after tea. Maybe take a short walk or listen to some music. That way, I'm still relaxing with her, but I'm not reaching for a cigarette. It seems okay, I suppose.

I think if we made it a routine to walk or listen to music after tea, it would become a habit. Maybe we could even start doing it on our own before she reminds me to quit smoking. That way, it would feel like something we do together, but also like something I'm doing for myself.

Maybe I could treat myself to a small reward after a few days or a week without smoking, like a favorite dish or a nice cup of coffee from the new shop downtown. Something that feels like a real privilege, not just a nag from my wife.

There was a day last week when a customer was being super annoying, and I really wanted to take a break and smoke. But I didn't. I took some deep breaths instead and focused on the sale. After the customer left, I felt a sense of accomplishment and pride in myself. That's the kind of moment I'd like to reward myself for.

I think I would like to treat myself to a new pen for my shop. A nice pen is something I've been wanting, but I've been putting it off. It would be a small thing, but it would feel like a real reward, and it's something I could use every day as a reminder of that difficult day when I resisted the urge to smoke.
I've been smoking for 20 years and I've tried to quit many times. I don't know why I'm even here. It never works out.
The anxiety. It's always the same - I feel like I'm on edge, my stomach's in knots, and I just can't relax. I've been like that for days after I try to quit, and it's just so hard to deal with.

One evening... I was at my sister's house, and we were watching a movie together. We had a big bowl of popcorn, and for some reason, I didn't even think about cigarettes. It was like my mind just forgot. I remember feeling really calm, just enjoying the movie and my sister's company. But it was just one evening, and it didn't last.

I think it was just being surrounded by people I love... my sister and her kids. It was a nice, quiet evening, and I wasn't feeling stressed or anxious like I often do at home in the evenings. And yeah, not thinking about cigarettes was a big part of it, too. It felt so... normal.
I don't know... I'm not sure I can just "imagine" feeling calm. It's hard to explain, but it feels like the anxiety is just always there, underneath everything. I'm not sure I can just turn it off.

It's definitely worse when I'm home alone in the evenings. That's when I usually reach for a cigarette, just out of habit, I guess. And it's not just the smoking, it's the whole routine that goes with it... watching TV, browsing my phone... it's just a really comfortable, familiar thing to do when I'm alone, but it feels like it's holding me back from being able to relax, you know?

I don't know... I've tried reading, but I get bored after a while. And I've tried going for walks, but it's hard to motivate myself to get out the door. I feel like I need something that's just as easy and convenient as watching TV, you know?

I have tried it a few times, but I only made it through like, 5 minutes before I got distracted and quit. I don't know... it just felt like too much work to keep myself from getting bored. But I suppose it's worth trying again?

I could try that. I've seen videos online where they do guided stretching and breathing, and it seems really calming. Maybe if I have someone else telling me what to do, I can stay focused. But what if I get bored with the videos, too? It's hard to commit to something that might not stick.
About 10 years now, since I was 24. It's just something I've gotten used to, you know? Everyone smokes during breaks, it's like a way to relax and catch up with each other.

I guess just the camaraderie, hanging out with the guys, sharing a laugh or two. Smoking's just something we do, but if you took that away, I don't know, it'd feel weird, I suppose. Like we're all doing something without doing it.

Actually, yeah... there was this one time, we were all out celebrating a friend's birthday, and we went to watch a cricket match together. We didn't smoke at all, we just hung out, ate some snacks, watched the game... it was actually pretty nice, I thought. But, I don't know, that was a special occasion, not just a regular day at the dhaba.

I think it was because everyone was in a good mood, you know? It was a celebration, so we were all relaxed and having fun, and the game was pretty interesting, too. We were all just caught up in it, cheering and stuff. I guess what I'm saying is, if we're all having fun, we can find other ways to enjoy each other's company that don't involve smoking.
Yeah, I'll try. It's not a big deal, and if it doesn't work out, we can always go back to smoking. But if it does, it might be something we can all enjoy together. I'm game to try, at least.

Yeah, that's not a bad idea. One of the guys, Rohan, he's actually a pretty good guy, we've been friends for years. If I'm feeling stuck, maybe I could call him, and he could help me find something else to do. That might actually work.

motivation_reason: should be able to handle it better

session_notes.note: I quit for about 3 months last year. Then a really bad week at work happened and I started again. Now I'm back to 15 a day.
It was the constant meetings and tight deadlines. I felt like I was going to lose my job over it. And my manager was breathing down my neck. That anxiety just took over, I guess.
Yeah, I still get anxious about deadlines and meetings, it's just part of my job. But I don't know, I feel like I should be able to handle it better now that I've quit before.

I guess it was just the feeling of deprivation, you know, not being able to smoke when I wanted to. And the withdrawal symptoms, my headaches were really bad at first. But I was surprised by how much it helped to have a routine, to do things like take long walks after work. It made the cravings feel more manageable.

Yeah, that could work. I could take short walks during breaks, or maybe even do some stretching exercises at my desk. It's just that when I'm in a meeting, I don't really have a chance to get up and walk around. What do you suggest I do during those times?

I could try that. But it's hard to focus on deep breathing when people are yelling at you about deadlines. Do you think there's any way to deal with that part of it, the meetings and the stress from the other people?

I guess I could take a few deep breaths before the meeting, but what if it's a super important one and I have to go right in? And what if I have to make a presentation? My anxiety is going to be through the roof. This is all just a lot to think about.

motivation_reason: fear of things spiraling out of control
try out that new yoga studio

session_notes.note: I smoke because it calms me down when I feel anxious. I know it makes things worse but I can't stop.

It's hard to explain... but when I have an anxiety episode, my heart starts racing and everything feels overwhelming. It's like my mind is a million miles an hour and I just can't catch my breath. Before I smoke, I feel like I'm going to have a panic attack. I know it's not a healthy reason to smoke, but it feels like the only thing that helps calm me down, even if it's just for a little while.

I'd say I smoke around 8 or 10 cigarettes a day. And... I've been smoking for about 5 years now. It's just become a part of my routine, I guess.  But like I said, I'm not really looking to quit or anything... I'm just here because my therapist said I should address it.

Honestly, I don't think I could do it. I mean, I tried to cut back once before, and it was a nightmare. My anxiety got so bad that I couldn't leave my house for days. I don't know... I feel like I'm already so behind compared to my friends, who all smoke and seem totally fine. I don't know if I'm ready to deal with all that on my own.

I don't know... I guess that's kind of what my therapist said before, but it's just hard to think about waiting it out. I feel like I'm already running out of time, you know? My anxiety is like this constant thing in the back of my mind, and I'm always worried that if I don't smoke, things are going to spiral out of control. But... I did have one day not too long ago where I was in a meeting and I started to feel anxious, and I just waited it out and it wasn't as bad as I thought it would be. It was actually kind of okay.

I mean, I could try that, I guess. It's not like it's a huge leap, right? Just waiting 10 minutes feels doable, but at the same time... it feels like I'm already setting myself up for failure. What if I'm still feeling anxious after 10 minutes? What if it's even worse? But... I also feel like if I can just get through that first 10 minutes, maybe I can start thinking more clearly and figure out how to deal with it without smoking. Yeah, maybe that's worth a shot.

I think taking a few deep breaths and going for a short walk might help. I've tried meditation before, but it's hard for me to focus. And just focusing on my breathing might actually be kind of calming. I don't know, it's not like I've really thought about it before, but maybe that's something I could try. Do you think that sounds like a good idea?

Yeah, actually... my partner does that sometimes, and it seems to really help her. She'll just get up and take a quick walk around the block, and it seems to calm her down. Maybe I could try that too... it's not like I'm a complete stranger to walking, but I guess I never thought to do it when I'm feeling anxious.

I... I think I can try that. It's not a huge change, but at least it's a new habit to form. And if it works, it's one less reason to smoke. I mean, that's the goal, right? So... yeah, let's say I'm going to try taking a walk instead of smoking when I feel anxious.
I... thank you. Just talking about this stuff is helping me feel a little more hopeful. Maybe I can actually do this. Maybe I don't have to be a smoker forever.

That's a nice idea... I think. I mean, I'm not great at rewarding myself, but if I'm being honest, it would be really nice to have something to look forward to. Maybe I could treat myself to a massage or something? I've been wanting to try out that new yoga studio too. Yeah, that sounds like a good idea.

I think if I had someone to go with me, that would make it more fun. Maybe my partner could join me, or one of my friends who doesn't smoke. That way it wouldn't feel so lonely, and I'd have someone to talk to afterwards. Yeah, that would be nice.
I had quit for 2 full years. Then I went to a party, had a few drinks, and one of my friends offered a cigarette. That was 6 months ago. Now I'm back to 10-15 a day.

I guess it's just who I've been around my whole life. My family, friends, we've all smoked together. It's just part of the culture, you know? And it feels... normal. Like, if I'm not smoking, I'm not really part of the group.
There's my sister. She's not a smoker, and we're really close. She's actually been trying to get me to quit for years. She's always been supportive, even when I went back to smoking.

Yeah, I think that's a good idea. It would be nice to have her by my side. But I don't know, it's not like I can just tell her to quit smoking for me too, right? I mean, it's hard enough for me to do it myself.

That sounds like a good way to do it. I can ask her to be my support person without putting any pressure on her. Just someone to text and say, "Hey, I'm at a party and I'm thinking about smoking, can you remind me why I don't want to?"

I guess I haven't really thought that far ahead. I mean, I know I'll try to say no, but what if she says something like, "Oh, come on, just one won't hurt"? Or if my friends ask me why I'm not smoking, I don't want to be like, "Oh, I'm trying to quit." I don't know, it just feels weird to say no without giving some kind of reason.
Yeah, that's not a bad idea. I don't have to do it out loud or anything, right? Just pretend. Okay, let me try it... "I'm trying to quit for my health." Yeah, that feels okay, I guess.

It's hard to say, but I think I'll feel... good, I guess. Like I'm in control of my own actions and I'm not trying to sneak around. It's a weird feeling, because I'm used to feeling like a smoker, you know? But I think it could be kind of liberating to be able to say no and mean it.

To be honest, I'm not sure. I know the morning cravings are usually the worst, and they're hard to fight. But if I can get through the morning, I think the rest of the day will be a lot easier. And maybe if I have my sister to text or something, it'll help me get through those tough moments.

past_strategies.strategy: roll down the window and take deep breaths

session_notes.note: I've tried to quit 3 times. The longest I went was 3 months. But I just can't seem to stay quit. My daughter is growing up and I don't want her to see me like this.

During those 3 months, I felt a little better, I guess. My breathing was a bit easier, and I didn't cough as much, but the cravings were still there. It just got to the point where I got tired of fighting it, and I started smoking again. When I'm not smoking, I feel so anxious, especially when I'm driving or in meetings. Those are my worst times.
I don't know... 15, I guess. Maybe a little more. It's hard to remember, but it wasn't like I was smoking any less when I quit. It was just... easier.

I think so. Yeah, I felt like I was in control when I was quit for 3 months. It was like I had won something, you know? But when I started smoking again, it was like I lost all that progress. My husband will sometimes smoke in the house, and it gets to me. He doesn't even realize it.

That sounds easy to say, but it's hard to ask him to do that. He doesn't think it's a big deal, and I don't want to be a nag or ruin our relationship. But maybe if I explain it in a different way... he's always saying I'm his support system, but I'm not really supporting myself right now, am I?

I don't know... I feel like he'll just tell me I'm overreacting or that it's no big deal. He doesn't understand why it bothers me so much. I've tried talking to him before, and it just ends up in an argument.

I'd have to take a deep breath and not get defensive, I guess. Maybe I could say something like, "Hey, I appreciate you smoking, but it's really hard for me when you do it in the house. Could we find a way to do it outside or somewhere else?" Instead of saying, "You need to stop smoking in the house."

I usually try to distract myself by singing along to the radio or something, but that doesn't always work. Sometimes I'll roll down the window and take some deep breaths, but it's hard to get any fresh air that way. I think what I really need is something to do with my hands... like a stress ball or something.

That's a good idea. I like to draw, and it can be calming. But I don't know if it would work in the car, since I won't be able to see what I'm doing very well. Maybe I could try to do some simple doodles in my head, or just concentrate on the lines and patterns on the steering wheel?
I think so. I mean, it's not going to be easy, but it sounds like a good place to start. And it's something I can control, which is what's been missing for me.

smoking_status_history: gutkha is part of my life

session_notes.note: I chew gutkha, not cigarettes. About 8 to 10 pouches a day for the last 7 years. My mouth has developed some kind of white patch and the doctor is worried.
Yes, I do. When I'm stuck in traffic or have long breaks between rides, I feel like I need to use it to calm down. Sometimes even after meals, I crave it.

That's not a bad idea. I used to doodle in school, it was fun. Maybe I can take a small notebook and pencil with me, and use it when I'm waiting for passengers. But what if I get cravings at other times, not just when I'm waiting?

It's not something I've ever thought of doing before. I usually just eat or chew gutkha when I'm bored or stressed. Talking to someone might actually help, though. It's just that it feels weird to share something like this with others, you know? It's like, they don't understand how much gutkha is just... part of my life.

That's a good idea. Writing it down might make me think more clearly about why I want to quit. I've been thinking about my health, obviously, because of the white patch and the doctor's warning. But I've also been thinking about my savings, I spend a lot of money on gutkha every day. It's around 150 rupees, that's a lot of money for little pouches.

No, I don't think so. I just know that I spend a lot on it. But now that you mention it, I think that would be a good idea. Knowing exactly how much I'm spending every day might make it feel more real, you know? Make me think about it more.

Yeah, that sounds like a good idea too. I can try to write it down, see when I usually buy gutkha. Maybe it's just when I stop for a meal or at the traffic light, like I said before. Writing it down might help me see those patterns more clearly.

I can try. It might be a bit more work, but if it helps me understand why I buy it, then it's worth it. It's just... sometimes I'm not really thinking about it, I just do it. But if I can figure out what's behind it, maybe I can break that habit.

Yeah, I think I can do that. One day without gutkha sounds manageable. It's just one day, I can make it through that. And if I can make it through one day, maybe it's not so hard to make it through another...

motivation_reason: losing my spot on the team

session_notes.note: I started smoking 2 years ago in college. My friends all do it. I'm on the cricket team and my stamina is getting really bad now.

Yeah, like, when I'm out there and feeling tired, I just think I need something to clear my head, you know? It's like my friends, they all do it before the game, so it feels like it should be helping, but... cricket coach says I'm losing my spot on the team, and that's really bothering me.
Not really... I mean, I've thought about it, but it's just easier to grab a cigarette, you know? And my friends, they all do it, so it feels normal. I don't want to be the one who can't keep up.

I don't know, like, my parents would freak out if they found out I smoke... I don't want to tell them. And I'm not really close to my family about this kind of stuff. But my coach is really supportive about quitting, he says it'll help my game... maybe I could talk to him about it?
Yeah, that's a good idea, I guess. Having him there would make it feel like less of a big deal, you know? But what if my friends are like "what's the catch?" or something?

He'd probably just tell them that it's good for my game, and that I'm trying to quit for health reasons. He's all about sportsmanship and being a team player... maybe they'd actually respect him for it.
Yeah, that sounds good. I think talking to him about it would be a good idea, see what he's willing to do. But what if he's busy, or... or he can't make it to every situation?

There's this one teammate, Rohan, he's always been a bit quieter than the rest of us, but he's always been supportive with me. He's never smoked, so maybe he could be someone to talk to if I'm feeling tempted.
That makes sense, I guess. It's not like I'm looking for someone to quit with, I just need someone to talk to when I'm feeling stuck.
Yeah, I can ask him that. It's not a huge ask, just having someone to listen I think would help.
My son brought me here. I smoke 4-5 bidis a day and eat paan. I've been doing this for 30 years. I don't think I can stop at this age.
After I finish cooking, I usually smoke a bidi. It's a habit, I guess. Helps me relax.
I don't know... my hands feel empty after eating. It's a habit, like I said. I'll just have one bidi, what's the harm? My son's always saying something, but...
Thank you. But I have to think about my afternoons too. I rest a lot in the afternoons and smoke paan. That's a big habit. I don't know if I can quit all of it at once.
One afternoon without paan... that's a start, I suppose. Can I have a replacement in case I get cravings, like a sweet or something?
It's usually after I take my nap, around 2-3 in the afternoon. That's when I feel like having paan.
I smoke about 15 cigarettes a day. And I drink on weekends. When I drink, the smoking goes up a lot. My wife has been asking me to stop both.

I've tried, but it's hard. When I'm drinking, I just want to smoke, you know? It feels like they go together. I don't know, maybe I could try gum or something, but I've heard that's not always effective.

Delaying it by a few minutes? That might work, I guess. But it's not like I can avoid drinking altogether; I've got friends I see on the weekends and we like to have a few beers. Can't just quit doing that.

I don't know, it's a hassle. We do it together because we like to. But maybe... maybe I could just have a couple of beers and not smoke as much. Is that like, a step in the right direction or something?

That sounds like a good idea, I think. Yeah, we could try that. I just don't know if it'll actually stick. I mean, if I'm feeling stressed or whatever, I'll probably just want to sit down and smoke anyway. But at least it's something to try, right?

Yeah, that's a good point. If I'm feeling stressed, maybe I could just take a couple of deep breaths first and see how I feel. It might not be easy, but if it helps me quit smoking, it's worth trying. Okay, I think I'm starting to get this. What comes next?
I've been smoking for about 2 years. My husband and I are planning to have a baby soon. I know I need to stop.

Work breaks, that's a big one. I always seem to grab a cigarette when I'm stressed, and teaching can be pretty stressful at times. And then, I guess, dinner with family or friends will be different, too.
I've tried walking, yeah, but it's not always easy. Sometimes I feel like I'm too anxious to get up and walk around, and even when I do, I'm still thinking about smoking.

It's kind of a combination of both, I guess. My mind starts racing, and I feel a little jittery inside. Sometimes I get a knot in my stomach, but I think that's more when I'm worried about something specific. When it's just anxiety, it's more like... I don't know, a feeling of being on edge, I suppose.
I'd be open to it, yeah. I just want to make sure I can handle quitting before I get pregnant. I don't want to have to go through this again if we're not successful.

I mean, it's not ideal because we want to have a baby soon, but if it means I can stop smoking for good, then yeah, that makes sense. Can you give me an idea of when I should start trying to quit, and how long I should stick to it?

If I can do it for a few months, it would mean a lot to me. I'd feel like I can really do it, you know? It would be a big confidence booster, and I think it would make a big difference in our decision to have a baby.
I could do something fun with my husband, like take a weekend trip or try a new restaurant. Or maybe I could buy a new outfit or something. Having something to look forward to would be nice.

motivation_reason: get more comfortable with the idea of taking a break from my tasks without smoking

session_notes.note: I work from home and since 2020 my smoking has more than doubled. I used to smoke 5 a day and now it's 18-20.

To be honest, I think it's the constant switching between work and personal tasks. I find myself taking breaks to smoke when I get overwhelmed or need a change of scenery, but there's no actual change of scenery when you're working from home.

It's usually after I've been stuck on a particular task for a while, I get frustrated and take a break to smoke. And then sometimes I'll use it as a reward to myself after completing a difficult task or at lunchtime, it's like a habitual thing now.

I don't know, it sounds easy to say, but actually doing it might be tough. I've tried this before and it didn't work out. I'd say I'm frustrated, wait 10 minutes, and then I'd just be even more frustrated because I still haven't solved the problem. Plus, I'd probably just find some other way to distract myself, like scrolling through my phone or something.

I've tried taking short walks before, but I'd usually just end up smoking afterwards anyway. But maybe this time, if I'm actually trying to focus on the walk and not smoking, it could be different. What if I, uh, set a goal to walk 300 steps every time I feel frustrated? Would that be a good starting point?

I can try. I've always been interested in mindfulness and meditation, but I've never actually made the time for it. Having a timer and a specific goal might make it more structured and less overwhelming. Does that mean I'll still be smoking at the same rate initially, and then try to eventually phase it out?

I think it could help me get more comfortable with the idea of taking a break from my tasks without smoking. It's funny, when I think about it, I've been trying to track my cigarette smoking to try and cut back, but that didn't work out. But the idea of tracking my breathing exercises and gradually increasing the time feels more manageable.

Yeah, that makes sense. Writing down my feelings might help me see some patterns I'm not even aware of. And if I'm being honest, it's not just about the smoking, it's about feeling more focused and productive when I work from home. If I can figure out how to manage my frustration and boredom, maybe that'll help with the smoking too.

There was one day last week when I was working on a project and I got stuck on a tricky problem. Instead of smoking, I decided to take a 5-minute walk and practiced my breathing exercises. When I came back, I was able to approach the problem with a fresh perspective and eventually solved it. It was a small win, but it felt good. I realized that I didn't need smoking to take a break and come back to my tasks feeling more focused. Maybe I can build on that.

Yeah, I feel like I'm starting to see some actual progress here. If I can recreate that scenario, maybe I can develop a new habit of dealing with frustration without smoking. It's not going to be easy, but I think I'm ready to try. What's the next step?
I used to smoke one and a half bundles of bidis a day. That's about 25. Now I'm down to 2 or 3. My doctor says I should stop completely.

It was the stress of work, I guess. And when I'd get frustrated because I'd forget things, like where I put my keys. Those were the times I'd smoke more. But I don't have that job stress anymore, so...
It's...less intense, yes. I still get frustrated, but I don't crave a bidi as much. I can manage it. But the morning one, the first bidi of the day, that's the hardest. Always has been.
I've tried walking, but it's winter now and the mornings are chilly, so I don't feel like going out. And it's not like I want to exercise, per se, I just want to...you know, smoke.

It's...possible. I haven't given in in a while, but it's still a possibility. I mean, I'm not like I'm 10 days into quitting, you know? I've been cutting down for months. It's just this last part. It's hard.
I'll feel like I've taken a step back, I guess. Frustrated with myself. But I've been trying not to be too hard on myself, just focus on the day after.
I think I need to. She's been patient with me for so long, I should be more open with her. Maybe it'll help me stay committed to quitting.
I smoke about 20 cigarettes a day. I calculate I spend about 4000 rupees a month on cigarettes. My family is struggling and I feel guilty about it.

Like when I come back home from a long delivery and I see my kids asking for money for school fees, I want to relax and sometimes I grab a cigarette. And then after meals too, it's a habit, like it's a reward or something.
I go to the kitchen and get a cup of chai. It's just a habit, I guess. But the thing is, chai tastes nice, but I still want to sit and relax, and then I think about having a cigarette.

I don't know, it sounds a bit too much effort. I mean, I'm tired after a long day, and walking to the living room just feels like extra work. But, I guess that 4000 rupees a month thing is still bothering me...

I can do that. I already fidget with my phone when I'm bored, why not a pen? But what about the smoking itself? Are we just gonna focus on not smoking for those few seconds or do we actually have a plan to cut down or quit for good?

Okay, I can try that. Saving money for my kids' education is a big goal for me. If I could cut down on the cigarettes, I'd have more money for that. But it's gonna be tough, I know I've tried before and I ended up going back to my usual habit.

It was a bad traffic jam on the way to a delivery, and I was so stressed out and anxious, and next thing I knew I was lighting up. My boss yelled at me for being late, my car was stuck in traffic, and I just lost my cool. That's when I started smoking again. It was just a really bad day, you know?

You know, that might actually work. I mean, when I'm stuck in traffic, it's easy to think about just giving up and smoking. But if I can focus on my kids and how much this is for them... maybe I can make it through the day without reaching for a cigarette.
Yeah, I can try that. Imagining myself saying no to a cigarette sounds better than actually doing it, I suppose. It's like I'm rehearsing for a tough situation. Yeah, okay, let's do it.

motivation_reason: feel like I can do this
treat myself to a new outfit

session_notes.note: I only smoke at parties or when I'm drinking with friends, maybe 3-4 times a week. It's not like I'm a real smoker. But I've been having asthma attacks more often.

I guess we could order more drinks or dance more, that usually helps me relax. And maybe I could offer to get the group something to eat, that usually keeps me occupied. But sometimes my friends will be smoking and it's just...I don't know, it feels like I'm missing out if I don't join them.

That might be okay, I don't know...I don't want to be that friend who can't hang out with everyone. But if it would help me stop smoking, maybe it would be worth it. What if my friends notice that I'm not smoking and they make a big deal about it?
I guess that might help. I just want to be able to hang out with them and not have it be a big deal. Like, I could just say I'm trying to take care of my asthma or something.
If I say it to myself enough times, maybe I'll start to believe it too. But what if someone asks me if I'm really quitting forever, and I say yes, but then I get a craving or something?
That sounds like something that might actually help, yeah. I'd rather say something like that to myself than beat myself up over it.

There's this one party coming up in two weeks, and my friends are going to be there, and...yeah, that's a good test case, I guess. If I can get through that without smoking, I might actually feel like I can do this.
That sounds like a good idea. Like, if I make it through the party without smoking, maybe I can treat myself to a new outfit or something. That would be a nice motivator.

I'd want it to be something pretty and not too casual, like a dressy top or a nice pair of boots, something I can wear to work or out with friends. It would be a good way to mark the occasion and feel proud of myself for trying to quit smoking.
